Stroke and PM&R Interactive Study Hub

A board-exam focused revision page covering definition, neuroanatomy, vascular territories, pathology, pathophysiology, stroke syndromes, neuroplasticity, acute-to-chronic rehabilitation, spasticity, cognition, language, and swallowing.

Study modules
Progress: 0%

Overview and how to use this page

Use this page like a clinical reasoning map. First, identify the vascular territory and lesion level. Then connect it to impairment, activity limitation, complications, and rehabilitation phase.

Core exam logic

Where is the lesion? Cortex, subcortex, brainstem, cerebellum, or spinal-like tract pattern.

What is impaired? Motor, sensory, tone, cognition, language, swallowing, vision, balance, mood.

What is the rehab phase? Acute, subacute, or chronic.

PM&R mindset

Stroke is not only hemiparesis. It is a disability syndrome involving function, participation, caregivers, environment, and secondary prevention.

Board exam shortcut

Always mention: diagnosis and localization, functional status, complications, goals, therapy prescription, safety, caregiver education, and follow-up.

One-line framework: lesion localization → impairment profile → functional limitation → complication risk → phase-based rehabilitation → measurable goal.

Checklist before moving on

1. Definition, types, and risk factors

Stroke is a medical emergency caused by interrupted blood flow to the brain due to blockage or bleeding, leading to brain cell injury, neurological deficit, disability, or death.

Ischemic stroke

Arterial occlusion reduces blood flow. Causes include large artery atherosclerosis, cardioembolism, small vessel disease, dissection, vasculitis, and hypercoagulable states.

Core + penumbratPA / EVT windowSecondary prevention

Hemorrhagic stroke

Rupture of a vessel causes intracerebral or subarachnoid bleeding. Damage comes from mass effect, edema, raised intracranial pressure, toxicity of blood products, and secondary ischemia.

ICHSAHBP control

TIA

Transient neurological deficit caused by brief ischemia without established infarction. Clinically important because early stroke risk can be high and urgent secondary prevention is needed.

Warning eventMRI DWI useful

Risk factors

ModifiableNon-modifiable or less modifiableRehab relevance
Hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, smoking, obesity, physical inactivity, unhealthy diet, alcohol, atrial fibrillation management, sleep apneaAge, prior stroke, family history, chronic kidney disease, pre-existing cardiac diseaseSecondary prevention must be integrated into rehab: exercise prescription, BP control, diabetes control, fall prevention, medication adherence, education.
Exam answer: definition of stroke
Stroke is an acute focal or global neurological deficit caused by vascular injury to the brain, retina, or spinal cord, either ischemic or hemorrhagic. In rehabilitation medicine, stroke should be described by lesion location, pathophysiology, impairment profile, activity limitation, participation restriction, and complication risk.

2. Basic neuroanatomy for stroke

Stroke localization becomes easier when you separate vascular territory, cortical function, subcortical tracts, brainstem nuclei, and cerebellar coordination.

Clickable vascular territory schematic

Click a colored territory.

Select a territory

Click ACA, MCA, PCA, lacunar, or vertebrobasilar territory to see the clinical pattern.

Circle of Willis schematic. Use this image to connect vessel anatomy with stroke syndromes: ACA and MCA dominate anterior circulation, PCA supplies posterior cortical territory, and vertebral-basilar flow supplies the brainstem, cerebellum, thalamus, and posterior cerebrum.

How to read the Circle of Willis clinically

The Circle of Willis is an arterial anastomotic ring at the brain base. In exams, do not memorize it only as anatomy. Use it as a localization map: anterior circulation usually produces cortical motor, sensory, language, and neglect syndromes, while posterior circulation commonly produces visual, cerebellar, brainstem, swallowing, and consciousness problems.

Click a vessel above. The explanation will link anatomy, common stroke syndrome, and rehabilitation priorities.
VesselMain territoryClinical cluePM&R implication
ACAMedial frontal and parietal lobesContralateral leg weakness more than arm, abulia, gait initiation difficulty, urinary symptomsGait initiation, balance, executive function, continence, fall prevention
MCALateral frontal, parietal, temporal cortex; deep lenticulostriate branchesFace-arm weakness more than leg; aphasia if dominant; neglect if non-dominant; dense motor deficit if internal capsuleUpper limb rehab, shoulder protection, aphasia or neglect management, ADL retraining
PCAOccipital lobe, inferior temporal lobe, thalamic branchesHomonymous hemianopia, visual agnosia, memory disorder, thalamic sensory painVisual scanning, safety education, cognitive compensation, pain management
Vertebral-basilarBrainstem, cerebellum, posterior circulationDiplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, vertigo, ataxia, crossed signs, altered consciousnessSwallow safety, cranial nerve assessment, balance and coordination training, airway and aspiration prevention
Lacunar perforatorsInternal capsule, basal ganglia, thalamus, ponsPure motor, pure sensory, sensorimotor, ataxic hemiparesis, dysarthria-clumsy handMotor recovery, gait, hand function, spasticity prevention, cardiovascular risk control

Cortical landmarks: medial and lateral views

Use the cortical surface as a clinical map. The medial surface helps you remember ACA syndromes because the paracentral lobule contains lower-limb motor and sensory representation. The lateral surface helps you remember MCA syndromes because the lateral frontal, parietal, and temporal cortices contain face-arm motor regions, language networks, and visuospatial networks.

Medial cerebral surface. High-yield stroke link: ACA territory involves the medial frontal-parietal region, so leg-predominant weakness, abulia, gait initiation difficulty, and urinary symptoms are logical. Source supplied by user: UTHealth Neuroanatomy Online.
Lateral cerebral surface. High-yield stroke link: MCA territory covers the lateral convexity, so face-arm weakness, aphasia in dominant hemisphere lesions, neglect in non-dominant hemisphere lesions, and visual field deficits are expected. Source supplied by user: UTHealth Neuroanatomy Online.
Localization shortcut. Medial cortex = ACA = leg and frontal initiation. Lateral cortex = MCA = face-arm, language, neglect. Occipital cortex = PCA = visual field and visual processing.

Brainstem anatomy: why posterior circulation stroke looks different

Midbrain cross-section. Brainstem strokes combine long tract signs with cranial nerve, arousal, ocular motor, and sensory findings. This is the anatomical reason for crossed signs: ipsilateral cranial nerve deficits with contralateral body motor or sensory deficits.

Clinical reading of brainstem stroke

In the brainstem, motor tracts, sensory lemnisci, reticular formation, cranial nerve nuclei, and cerebellar connections are tightly packed. Therefore small lesions can produce complex syndromes.

  • Midbrain: ocular motor deficits, altered consciousness, ataxia, contralateral weakness.
  • Pons: facial weakness, gaze palsy, dysarthria, ataxia, corticospinal weakness.
  • Medulla: dysphagia, hoarseness, vertigo, ataxia, crossed sensory findings, autonomic or respiratory concerns.

Descending motor systems: pyramidal and extrapyramidal tracts

The corticospinal tract explains voluntary weakness after stroke, while corticobulbar involvement explains dysarthria, facial weakness, and swallowing problems. Extrapyramidal brainstem pathways help explain abnormal tone, postural bias, synergy patterns, and impaired automatic balance responses after upper motor neuron lesions.

Corticospinal pathway schematic. A lesion above the pyramidal decussation produces contralateral weakness. A compact lesion in the posterior limb of the internal capsule can cause dense hemiparesis because corticospinal fibers are tightly packed. Source supplied by user: King's College London eHealth.

Interactive tract selector

Click a tract to connect anatomy with clinical stroke signs and PM&R reasoning.

Click a tract above. The explanation will show the origin, main function, and stroke-rehabilitation relevance.
SystemTractMain functionStroke and PM&R relevance
Pyramidal / voluntary motor controlLateral corticospinal tractVoluntary, highly skilled, precise movement of contralateral limbs.Lesion causes contralateral weakness, loss of fractionated movement, impaired dexterity, and later UMN signs. Rehab focus: task-specific motor relearning, strength, gait, hand function, and prevention of learned non-use.
Anterior corticospinal tractVoluntary movement and postural control of axial or trunk muscles.Lesion contributes to poor trunk control, impaired sitting balance, poor transfers, and reduced anticipatory postural adjustment.
Corticobulbar / corticonuclear tractVoluntary control of head and neck muscles through cranial nerve motor nuclei.Lesion contributes to facial weakness, dysarthria, dysphagia, impaired cough, and oral motor dysfunction. Rehab focus: swallow safety, speech therapy, oral motor and respiratory-cough strategy.
Extrapyramidal / posture, tone, reflexesRubrospinal tractOriginates from the red nucleus; facilitates flexor muscles and inhibits extensor muscles, especially in upper limb-biased motor control.Helps explain abnormal flexor bias and synergy expression when corticospinal control is reduced.
Lateral reticulospinal tractOriginates mainly from the medulla; facilitates flexor reflexes and inhibits extensor muscles, modulating tone.Loss of balanced corticoreticular control contributes to abnormal tone, spasms, and impaired automatic postural response.
Medial reticulospinal tractOriginates mainly from the pons; facilitates extensor muscles and supports posture.Overactivity can contribute to extensor synergy, lower limb extensor tone, equinovarus tendency, and stiff gait pattern after stroke.
Lateral vestibulospinal tractOriginates from vestibular nuclei; facilitates antigravity extensor activity for postural adjustment.Important for balance, stance stability, extensor tone, and falls risk, especially in brainstem and cerebellar involvement.
Medial vestibulospinal tractOriginates from vestibular nuclei; stabilizes head position and coordinates head-eye movement.Lesion or imbalance contributes to dizziness, impaired gaze stability, poor head control, and vestibular-balance problems.
Tectospinal tractOriginates from the superior colliculus; reflexive turning of head and neck toward visual or auditory stimuli.Relevant for orienting response, visual scanning, environmental awareness, and protective head-neck reaction.
Hypothalamospinal tractDescending autonomic pathway mediating sympathetic and parasympathetic-related responses such as pupillary dilation and sweating.Brainstem or cervical lesions may produce autonomic signs. In stroke localization, consider Horner syndrome, sweating asymmetry, temperature dysregulation, and autonomic instability.
Why spastic synergy appears after stroke
Synergy patterns appear because voluntary corticospinal fractionation is reduced, while brainstem pathways, especially reticulospinal and vestibulospinal influences, become relatively dominant. The patient loses selective movement and instead recruits mass flexor or extensor patterns. This is why treatment should not only “reduce tone,” but also train selective voluntary control, postural alignment, sensory feedback, task-specific movement, and functional carryover.
How this helps exam localization
Pure corticospinal involvement suggests motor weakness without cortical signs, often lacunar internal capsule, corona radiata, or pontine lesions. Corticobulbar involvement suggests dysarthria, facial weakness, and dysphagia. Brainstem tract plus cranial nerve involvement suggests posterior circulation. Cortical language or neglect signs suggest MCA cortical territory rather than a pure tract lesion.

Cortical versus subcortical signs

PatternSuggestsCommon signs
Cortical strokeFrontal, parietal, temporal, occipital cortexAphasia, neglect, apraxia, gaze deviation, visual field defect, cortical sensory loss, seizures.
Subcortical strokeInternal capsule, corona radiata, basal ganglia, thalamusDense motor or sensory signs without aphasia or neglect, depending on tract involvement.
Brainstem strokeMidbrain, pons, medullaCrossed signs, cranial nerve involvement, diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria, ataxia, altered consciousness.
Cerebellar strokePICA, AICA, SCA territoryAtaxia, vertigo, vomiting, nystagmus, dysmetria, dysarthria, risk of edema and brainstem compression.

Internal capsule high-yield anatomy

PartMain fibersLesion effect
Anterior limbFrontopontine fibers, thalamocortical connectionsExecutive dysfunction, abulia, behavioral change, less pure motor dominance.
GenuCorticobulbar fibersDysarthria, facial weakness, swallowing involvement.
Posterior limbCorticospinal and sensory fibersDense contralateral hemiparesis and sensory impairment.

3. Pathology and pathophysiology

The acute neurological deficit is only the start. The later disability depends on lesion location, infarct volume, edema, diaschisis, complications, neuroplasticity, and rehabilitation exposure.

Ischemic cascade

1. Arterial occlusion
Reduced cerebral blood flow.
→
2. ATP failure
Na/K pump failure, depolarization.
→
3. Excitotoxicity
Glutamate release, calcium influx.
→
4. Cellular injury
Mitochondrial failure, free radicals, inflammation.
→
5. Infarction
Core injury, penumbra may be salvageable early.

Ischemic core

Severely ischemic tissue with irreversible injury if reperfusion is not restored rapidly. This explains why acute stroke is time-sensitive.

Penumbra

Functionally impaired but potentially salvageable tissue. Imaging and acute reperfusion treatment aim to rescue this region.

Hemorrhagic stroke injury

Vessel rupture
ICH or SAH.
→
Mass effect
Compression and raised ICP.
→
Perihematomal edema
Inflammation and local injury.
→
Secondary injury
Hydrocephalus, herniation risk, secondary ischemia.
Diaschisis and early spontaneous recovery
Diaschisis is reduced function in remote but connected brain regions after a focal lesion. Some early recovery occurs because edema, inflammation, and diaschisis improve, not only because new motor skills are learned.
Why symptoms may worsen after initial stroke
Clinical worsening can occur from infarct extension, cerebral edema, hemorrhagic transformation, raised intracranial pressure, seizures, infection, hypoxia, glucose disturbance, hypotension, or new embolic events.

4. Stroke syndromes and clinical localization

Use motor distribution, cortical signs, cranial nerve findings, and cerebellar signs to localize the lesion.

Anterior circulation

SyndromeClinical patternLikely lesion
ACA syndromeContralateral leg weakness more than arm, abulia, gait initiation difficulty, urinary incontinence.Anterior cerebral artery.
MCA superior divisionFace and arm weakness more than leg, gaze preference, Broca aphasia if dominant hemisphere.Frontal lateral cortex.
MCA inferior divisionWernicke aphasia if dominant hemisphere; neglect and visuospatial deficit if non-dominant hemisphere.Temporal and inferior parietal cortex.
Large MCA / ICADense hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, hemianopia, gaze deviation, aphasia or neglect.Large anterior circulation stroke.
Lacunar pure motorContralateral face, arm, and leg weakness without cortical signs.Posterior limb internal capsule, corona radiata, or pons.

Posterior circulation

SyndromeClinical patternLikely lesion
PCA strokeHomonymous hemianopia, visual agnosia, alexia without agraphia if dominant splenial involvement.Occipital and inferior temporal territory.
Thalamic strokeHemisensory loss, central post-stroke pain, arousal or cognitive changes.Thalamus.
Lateral medullary syndromeDysphagia, hoarseness, vertigo, ataxia, ipsilateral facial pain-temperature loss, contralateral body pain-temperature loss.PICA or vertebral artery.
Medial medullary syndromeContralateral hemiparesis, contralateral proprioceptive loss, ipsilateral tongue weakness.Anterior spinal artery.
Cerebellar strokeVertigo, vomiting, ataxia, dysmetria, nystagmus, dysarthria; monitor for edema.PICA, AICA, or SCA.
Localization rule: cortical signs suggest cortex; pure motor or pure sensory signs suggest lacunar/subcortical lesion; crossed cranial nerve and long tract signs suggest brainstem.
Dominant versus non-dominant hemisphere
Dominant hemisphere, usually left, is associated with language: Broca, Wernicke, conduction, and global aphasia. Non-dominant hemisphere, usually right, is associated with neglect, anosognosia, visuospatial dysfunction, dressing apraxia, and impaired safety awareness.
Why brainstem stroke often produces crossed signs
Cranial nerve nuclei or fascicles are affected ipsilaterally, while corticospinal or spinothalamic tracts may already have crossed or will cross later. This creates ipsilateral cranial nerve signs with contralateral body signs.

5. Neuroplasticity after stroke

Recovery depends on biological recovery and experience-dependent learning. Rehabilitation should create the right input for adaptive plasticity.

Spontaneous biological recovery

Occurs especially in the early weeks to months due to resolution of edema, reperfusion of hypoperfused tissue, reduced inflammation, and improvement of diaschisis.

Training-induced plasticity

Driven by repetition, task specificity, salience, feedback, intensity, progressive challenge, and sufficient rest.

Principles of neuroplastic rehabilitation

PrincipleClinical translation
Use it or lose itAvoid learned non-use of the affected limb.
Use it and improve itTrain the impaired function, not only compensation.
SpecificityWalking improves with walking-related training; hand use improves with reaching, grasping, and release practice.
Repetition mattersHigh-repetition task practice is usually needed for motor learning.
Intensity mattersProvide enough challenge while respecting fatigue, cognition, medical stability, and safety.
Salience mattersMeaningful tasks improve engagement and carryover.
Transference and interferenceOne skill may generalize, but maladaptive compensation may also interfere with better movement patterns.
Important: motor recovery and spasticity are not the same. Adaptive neuroplasticity supports functional recovery, while maladaptive spinal and brainstem excitability contributes to spasticity and abnormal synergies.

6. PM&R management from acute to chronic phase

Stroke rehabilitation should start as soon as medically safe and continue as long as it helps the patient achieve meaningful goals.

Acute phase: hours to days

PriorityPM&R actionReason
Medical stabilityReview stroke type, vitals, oxygenation, BP, glucose, consciousness, imaging, contraindications.Prevent secondary brain injury and unsafe mobilization.
Stroke unit careWork with interdisciplinary team: physician, nurse, PT, OT, SLP, dietitian, psychologist, social worker, orthotics.Organized stroke unit care improves outcomes.
Swallow safetyScreen before oral intake; SLP assessment if abnormal; manage nutrition and medication route.Prevents aspiration pneumonia and malnutrition.
PositioningHead, trunk, scapula, shoulder, pelvis, hand, hip, ankle alignment.Prevents shoulder pain, edema, contracture, pressure injury.
Early mobilizationSit out of bed, stand, or walk when clinical condition permits. Avoid high-intensity mobilization in first 24 hours if the patient needs help to sit, stand, or walk.Reduces complications while avoiding harmful over-intensity.
Complication preventionROM, pressure care, DVT prevention, bowel/bladder monitoring, shoulder protection, delirium prevention.Early complications affect later recovery.

Subacute phase: days to around 6 months

GoalInterventionsMeasurement
Motor recoveryTask-specific training, progressive strengthening, balance, trunk control, sit-to-stand, gait training.FMA, MRC/MMT, TIS, BBS, 10MWT, 6MWT, FAC.
Upper limb functionReaching, grasp-release, bilateral training, CIMT if appropriate, mirror therapy, NMES trial when contraction exists.ARAT, Box and Block, FMA-UE, functional tasks.
ADLOT training: grooming, dressing, bathing, feeding, toileting, home simulation.Barthel Index, FIM, mRS, COPM.
CommunicationAphasia and dysarthria therapy, alternative communication aids, family communication training.SLP assessment, functional communication goals.
SwallowingDiet modification, maneuvers, CTAR, effortful swallow, oral care, FEES/VFSS when needed.FOIS, PAS on instrumental assessment, nutrition status.
Cognition and moodScreen cognition, attention, memory, neglect, executive function, depression, anxiety.MoCA, LOTCA, PHQ-9, HADS, neglect tests.
Intensity principle: if the patient can participate, multidisciplinary needs-based rehabilitation should be provided with adequate daily intensity, adjusted for fatigue, cognition, mood, medical stability, and goals.

Chronic phase: after 6 months

FocusExamples
ParticipationCommunity mobility, return to work, driving counseling, hobbies, family roles, sexuality.
Long-term physical conditioningAerobic training, resistance training, balance and fall prevention, community exercise.
Spasticity and painGoal-directed spasticity plan, botulinum toxin for focal spasticity, oral baclofen for generalized spasticity, shoulder pain management.
Orthosis and technology reviewAFO, FES for foot drop, wheelchair, adaptive equipment, communication aids.
Secondary preventionBP, diabetes, lipids, antithrombotic adherence, smoking cessation, physical activity, nutrition.
Caregiver and psychosocial healthCaregiver burden, depression, anxiety, social isolation, long-term follow-up.
30-minute clinical examination sequence for stroke
  1. Sitting: history, onset, vascular risk factors, handedness, communication, cognition screen, cranial nerve screen, sitting balance, trunk control, shoulder inspection, upper limb motor and tone.
  2. Supine: ROM, tone with velocity, reflexes, sensation, lower limb strength, contracture check, shoulder pain and subluxation safety.
  3. Standing: transfer, balance, weight bearing, postural alignment, gait if safe, AFO need, fall risk.
  4. End: summarize lesion, impairment, functional level, complication risk, goals, rehab plan, and caregiver education.

7. Spasticity after stroke

Spasticity is a velocity-dependent increase in stretch reflexes after upper motor neuron injury, but not all stiffness is spasticity.

Positive UMN signs

Spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski sign, flexor/extensor spasms, abnormal synergies.

Negative UMN signs

Weakness, poor selective motor control, loss of dexterity, fatigability, impaired motor planning.

Mechanism

Corticospinal and corticoreticular injury
Reduced descending inhibitory control.
→
Brainstem pathway imbalance
Increased reticulospinal influence.
→
Spinal reflex hyperexcitability
Reduced reciprocal inhibition and increased alpha motor neuron excitability.
→
Muscle soft-tissue change
Shortening, contracture, stiffness, pain.

Common patterns

Upper limbLower limbFunctional problem
Shoulder adduction/internal rotation, elbow flexion, forearm pronation, wrist and finger flexion, thumb-in-palm.Hip adduction/internal rotation, knee extension or flexion, ankle plantarflexion/inversion, toe flexion.Hygiene difficulty, pain, gait instability, poor brace fit, contracture, sleep disturbance, reduced ADL.

Goal-directed management algorithm

  1. Define the problem: pain, hygiene, gait, sleep, positioning, ROM, function.
  2. Search for triggers: UTI, constipation, pressure injury, pain, poor orthosis, fracture, ingrown nail.
  3. Measure: MAS, Tardieu, passive ROM, active function, gait/ADL impact.
  4. Start conservative: positioning, stretching, active movement, task practice, splinting when indicated.
  5. Focal spasticity: botulinum toxin, phenol/alcohol neurolysis, NMES/FES/TENS trial, serial casting when appropriate.
  6. Generalized spasticity: oral baclofen or other systemic agents with adverse-effect monitoring.
  7. Complex or refractory cases: specialist spasticity service, intrathecal baclofen, orthopedic or neurosurgical options.
Clinical reasoning: mild extensor tone may help standing or transfers. Treat spasticity when it causes harm or blocks goals, not merely because tone is present.
MAS versus Tardieu
Modified Ashworth Scale grades resistance during passive movement but cannot clearly separate neural spasticity from fixed soft-tissue stiffness. Tardieu compares slow and fast stretch, records catch angle, and better reflects velocity-dependent spasticity.
When to think contracture rather than spasticity
If resistance is present equally during slow and fast stretch, if passive ROM is fixed, or if there is hard end-feel, pain, or long-standing immobility, contracture or structural stiffness may dominate.

8. Cognitive and behavioral function after stroke

Cognitive impairment after stroke affects therapy participation, safety, carryover, ADL independence, and caregiver burden.

DomainClinical manifestationRehab strategy
AttentionDistractibility, poor task persistence.Reduce distractions, short sessions, cueing, graded attention training.
Executive functionImpulsivity, poor planning, poor safety judgment.Metacognitive strategies, structured routines, safety training, caregiver supervision.
MemoryPoor carryover, repeated questions.External aids, diary, alarms, errorless learning, routines.
NeglectIgnores contralesional body or space, unsafe wheelchair or gait.Visual scanning, limb activation, prism adaptation, functional task training.
ApraxiaDifficulty performing learned movement despite strength.Gesture training, object-based practice, stepwise cueing.
AnosognosiaPoor insight into deficits.Safety education, feedback, caregiver training, environmental controls.
MoodDepression, anxiety, emotionalism.Screen, educate, psychological support, medication when indicated.

Common tools

MoCA

Global cognitive screen, more sensitive than MMSE for executive dysfunction.

LOTCA

Orientation, visual perception, spatial perception, praxis, visuomotor organization, thinking operations.

Neglect tests

Line bisection, cancellation, Catherine Bergego Scale.

Mood

PHQ-9, HADS, GAD-7, clinical interview.

Why cognition changes the motor rehab plan
A patient with good strength but poor insight, neglect, or impulsivity may be less safe than a weaker patient with good awareness. Treatment must integrate attention, perception, safety, and caregiver training into gait and ADL sessions.

9. Language and communication after stroke

Communication impairment includes aphasia, dysarthria, and apraxia of speech. Aphasia is a language disorder, not dementia.

DisorderCore problemClinical clue
AphasiaLanguage formulation or comprehension.Impaired naming, fluency, comprehension, repetition, reading, or writing.
DysarthriaMotor execution of speech.Slurred, weak, spastic, ataxic, or hypokinetic speech pattern.
Apraxia of speechMotor planning of speech.Inconsistent sound errors, groping, sequencing difficulty.

Aphasia localization

TypeFluencyComprehensionRepetitionLikely lesion
BrocaNon-fluentRelatively preservedImpairedDominant inferior frontal gyrus.
WernickeFluent but often meaninglessImpairedImpairedDominant posterior superior temporal gyrus.
ConductionFluentRelatively preservedMarkedly impairedArcuate fasciculus or supramarginal region.
GlobalNon-fluentImpairedImpairedLarge dominant MCA.
Transcortical motorNon-fluentPreservedPreservedFrontal watershed.
Transcortical sensoryFluentImpairedPreservedTemporoparietal watershed.

Supportive communication

  • Use short phrases, keywords, gestures, pictures, writing, and yes/no boards.
  • Give enough response time and do not interrupt.
  • Verify understanding; do not assume cognitive impairment from poor speech output.
  • Train family and staff in communication strategies.
  • Consider communication aids and computer/app-based word-finding therapy as adjuncts.
Exam sentence: In aphasia, rehabilitation aims not only to restore language but also to preserve autonomy, decision-making, social interaction, identity, and participation.

10. Swallowing function and dysphagia after stroke

Dysphagia is common after stroke and can cause aspiration pneumonia, malnutrition, dehydration, medication problems, and delayed rehabilitation.

Swallowing phases

PhaseMain physiologyStroke problem
Oral preparatoryBolus acceptance, mastication, lip seal, tongue control.Drooling, pocketing, poor bolus formation.
Oral propulsiveTongue pushes bolus posteriorly.Delayed propulsion, oral residue.
PharyngealSwallow trigger, laryngeal elevation, airway closure, pharyngeal contraction.Aspiration before/during/after swallow, wet voice, weak cough.
EsophagealUES opening and esophageal transit.Residue, regurgitation, aspiration after swallow if severe.

Assessment and management pathway

Screen before oral intake
No food, fluid, or oral medication until safe.
→
SLP assessment
Cranial nerve, cough, voice, oral motor, trial swallows.
→
Instrumental if needed
FEES or VFSS for aspiration, residue, unclear physiology.
→
Intervention
Position, texture, maneuvers, exercises, oral care, nutrition plan.
InterventionExamplesPurpose
CompensationUpright position, small bolus, slow rate, chin tuck, head turn, texture modification.Immediate safety during eating and drinking.
ManeuversEffortful swallow, supraglottic swallow, Mendelsohn maneuver.Improve airway protection and bolus clearance.
ExercisesCTAR, Shaker, tongue strengthening, expiratory muscle strength training.Improve swallowing physiology over time.
Oral careTooth/gum brushing, mouth care assistance, manage dry mouth and infection.Reduce bacterial load and aspiration pneumonia risk.
NutritionNGT if unsafe or inadequate intake; PEG if prolonged need after team discussion.Maintain hydration, nutrition, and medication delivery.
Why semi-solid food may be safer in dysphagia
Semi-solid textures may move more slowly and cohesively than thin liquids, giving more time for airway closure and reducing premature spillage. However, safety depends on the specific swallowing impairment; residue risk may increase in patients with weak pharyngeal clearance.

11. Assessment tools and outcome measures

Choose measures based on the clinical question: impairment, activity limitation, participation, safety, or caregiver need.

DomainToolsUse
Stroke severityNIHSSAcute neurological deficit severity.
Global disabilitymRS, FIM, Barthel IndexFunctional independence and disability level.
Motor impairmentFugl-Meyer Assessment, MRC/MMT, Brunnstrom stageMotor recovery and impairment staging.
ToneMAS, Modified Tardieu Scale, ROM, Goal Attainment ScalingSpasticity severity, contracture distinction, goal response.
Balance and gaitBerg Balance Scale, TUG, 10MWT, 6MWT, FACFall risk, gait speed, endurance, walking independence.
Upper limbARAT, Box and Block, Nine-Hole Peg Test, FMA-UEReach, grasp, dexterity, hand function.
CognitionMoCA, LOTCA, Trail Making, neglect testsCognitive domains affecting function and safety.
SwallowingBedside swallow screen, FOIS, FEES, VFSSOral intake safety and swallowing physiology.
CommunicationSLP language assessment, functional communication measuresAphasia, dysarthria, communication participation.
ParticipationCOPM, Stroke Impact Scale, return-to-work measuresPatient-centered goals and quality of life.
Exam tip: Always match the tool to the question. Do not use only MMT for a complex stroke patient with neglect, aphasia, dysphagia, and caregiver burden.

12. Clinical case mode

Practice rapid localization and PM&R reasoning. Select a case, answer mentally, then reveal the explanation.

Case 1

A right-handed patient has acute right face and arm weakness, non-fluent speech, relatively preserved comprehension, and impaired repetition.

Reveal answer
Localization: dominant left MCA superior division, involving Broca area and adjacent motor cortex. PM&R issues: right hemiparesis, Broca aphasia, dysarthria/apraxia of speech differential, shoulder protection, ADL training, SLP therapy.

Case 2

A patient has left hemiparesis, severe left neglect, anosognosia, poor safety awareness, and visuospatial difficulty.

Reveal answer
Localization: non-dominant right MCA, often inferior parietal involvement. PM&R issues: neglect-aware transfer/gait training, fall prevention, caregiver supervision, visual scanning, prism or task-specific neglect interventions.

Case 3

A patient has dysphagia, hoarseness, vertigo, ipsilateral facial sensory loss, contralateral body pain-temperature loss, and ataxia.

Reveal answer
Localization: lateral medullary syndrome, usually PICA or vertebral artery. PM&R issues: severe dysphagia risk, aspiration prevention, balance/ataxia therapy, SLP assessment, nutrition route.

Case 4

A patient has pure left face-arm-leg weakness without aphasia, neglect, visual field deficit, or cortical sensory loss.

Reveal answer
Localization: lacunar pure motor stroke, often right posterior limb internal capsule, corona radiata, or pons. PM&R issues: motor recovery, tone prevention, gait training, AFO need, task-specific strengthening.

Create your own case

Type symptoms below and use the prompt to reason through localization.

Reasoning prompt: Are there cortical signs? Is it pure motor/sensory? Are there crossed cranial nerve signs? Is the leg more affected than arm? Is there ataxia out of proportion to weakness?

13. Flashcards

Click each card to flip. These are designed for rapid oral exam recall.

14. Quiz hub

Answer the questions and read the explanation. The questions mix localization, pathophysiology, and rehabilitation.

15. Key references

These sources were used to structure the study content. For exam reading, prioritize guidelines and standard rehabilitation textbooks.

  1. World Health Organization. Stroke fact sheet. Updated 19 December 2025.
  2. NICE Guideline NG128. Stroke and transient ischaemic attack in over 16s: diagnosis and initial management. Published 2019, last updated 2022, reviewed 2026.
  3. NICE Guideline NG236. Stroke rehabilitation in adults. Published 18 October 2023.
  4. Winstein CJ, Stein J, Arena R, et al. Guidelines for Adult Stroke Rehabilitation and Recovery. Stroke. 2016;47:e98-e169.
  5. Braddom's Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation. Stroke rehabilitation chapters.
  6. DeLisa's Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation: Principles and Practice. Stroke rehabilitation chapters.
  7. Kandel ER et al. Principles of Neural Science. Motor systems and neuroplasticity foundations.
  8. Mukherjee A, Chakravarty A. Spasticity mechanisms, for the clinician. Front Neurol. 2010.
This page is for study and exam preparation. Always adapt clinical decisions to local protocols, patient status, and specialist guidance.
